content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
May 18, 1999
Duane L. Cronk
Post Office Box 707
Angwin, California 94508
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-080
Dear Mr. Cronk:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Since your request asks for general
guidance, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has
afready taken place.
3 In addition, this letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in your
letter, and in the telephone conversations that we had on April 5, 1999, and May 3, 1999. The
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and
that all of the material facts have been provided.
Finally, you should keep in mind that the Commission’s jurisdiction is limited to the
provisions ofthe Act. We therefore cannot advise you regarding the impact that other laws, such
as the doctrine of incompatible activities, may have on your situation.
4
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; and Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
Pursuant to regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).)
For advice about whether the doctrine of incompatible activities applies to your situation, you should
contact the attorney for your school district.


File No. 1-99-080
Page 2
QUESTION
Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit you from participating, as an
unpaid volunteer, in the Howell Mountain Elementary School Board’s public information
program, while you are a candidate for election to the Board?
CONCLUSION
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act would not prohibit you from participating,
as an unpaid volunteer, in the Howell Mountain Elementary School Board’s public information
program, while you are a candidate for election to the Board.
FACTS
The Howell Mountain Elementary School Board (the “Board”) is instituting a public
information program, and has created a Communication Committee (the “Committee”) to
undertake this program. The Committee is composed of two members ofthe Board.
To carry out the purposes of the public information program, the Committee intends to
publish a monthly newsletter, and distribute copies ofthe newsletter to members of the
community. The newsletter will report on solutions to financial problems faced by the Board,
significant Board actions, and school activities. You wish to assist the Committee with the
newsletter, as an unpaid volunteer, although you will not be a member of the Committee.
Your role in the publication ofthe newsletter would be to write a draft copy ofthe
newsletter, participate in rewriting the draft as directed by the Committee, design a logo, and
arrange for mailing the final version ofthe newsletter to members of the community. The
Committee would determine the subject matters covered in the newsletter, and participate in
rewriting your draft to assure the newsletter’s accuracy. The Committee would have the ultimate
responsibility for the content ofthe newsletter.
After the Committee decided to publish the newsletter, but before you provided any
assistance with the newsletter’s actual publication, you filed papers to run for a vacant seat on the
Board.
You would now like to know whether you are prohibited from participating in the
publication of the newsletter while you are a candidate for the Board.


File No. 1-99-080
Page 3
ANALYSIS
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act are found at Section 87100, et seq. Section
87100 provides that:
“No public official at any level of state or local government shall
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.”
[emphasis added.)
The term “public official” is defined by Section 82048 ofthe Act, as follows:
“Public official’ means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not
include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of
government.”
According to the definition of “public official” contained in Section 82048 ofthe Act,
you are not a public official. As a mere candidate for election to the Board, you do not satisfy
the definition for a public official. (Knight Advice Letter, No. 1-98-183.) As a non-member
volunteer to the Committee, who is not a member, officer, employee, or consultant ofthe Board,
you do not satisfy the definition for a public official. (Andrus Advice Letter, No. A-95-163.)
Accordingly, the conflict of interest provisions ofthe Act do not apply to you, and they do not
have any bearing on whether you may participate in the Howell Mountain Elementary School
Board’s public information program while you are a candidate for the Board.
Regarding publication of the newsletter, however, while the Act’s conflict of interest
provisions do not apply to your situation, other provisions of the Act may be applicable to the
newsletter’s publication. If any issues of the newsletter expressly advocate your election to the
Board, the Board’s expenditures for the production ofthose issues may be considered either
contributions to, or independent expenditures on behalf of, your campaign. (See Sections 82015
and 82025, Regulations 18215 and 18225, and the Zuchelli Advice Letter, No. A-92-19, copies
enclosed.) If any issues of the newsletter feature any members ofthe Board (including you, if
you are elected to the Board), the newsletter may, under certain circumstances, constitute a
prohibited mass mailing at public expense. (See Sections 82041.5 and 89001, Regulation 18901,
and the White-Brown Advice Letter, No. A-99-073, copies enclosed.) We suggest that you stay
alert to these provisions ofthe Act in the production of the newsletter. We also encourage you to
contact us when any specific questions concerning the Act arise as you produce the newsletter.


File No. 1-99-080
Page 4
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Steven Benito Russo
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit you from participating, as an
unpaid volunteer, in the Howell Mountain Elementary School Board’s public information
program, while you are a candidate for election to the Board?

CONCLUSION: The conflict of interest provisions of the Act would not prohibit you from participating,
as an unpaid volunteer, in the Howell Mountain Elementary School Board’s public information
program, while you are a candidate for election to the Board.
